upcoming.name: Coach Note -- MSR Strength & Mobility
upcoming.description:
New This Week: MSR Strength & Mobility Sessions

You'll notice four new recurring sessions in your training plan -- two strength and two mobility. These are part of your marathon preparation and are designed to be done alongside your running, not instead of it.

WHY STRENGTH?
The two strength sessions (MSR Strength 1 and MSR Strength 2) target the muscles that keep your form intact when you're tired -- glutes, hips, core, and upper back. These are the things that break down in the back half of a race: knee drift, hip drop, collapsed posture, shortened stride. Twenty minutes of consistent work here will make a real difference come race day.

WHY MOBILITY?
The two mobility sessions (MSR Mobility 1 and MSR Mobility 2) focus on the areas runners chronically neglect -- hip flexors, glutes, calves, feet, and thoracic spine. Tightness in these areas reduces your running economy and increases injury risk over a long campaign. Done consistently, this work keeps you moving well and recovering faster between sessions.

TECHNIQUE MATTERS MORE THAN LOAD
These sessions are not about how heavy, how many, or how fast. They are about control. A half-hearted glute bridge does nothing -- a slow, deliberate one with a proper squeeze at the top is worth ten sloppy ones. Read the exercise descriptions carefully, move with intention, and if something doesn't feel right, slow down further. The benefits come from quality of movement, not quantity of effort.

WHAT YOU NEED
A resistance band, a foam roller, and a spiky ball. That's it.

WHEN TO DO THEM
The sessions are placed in your calendar to fit around your running each week, but if your schedule changes keep these principles in mind:

- Strength 1 is glute and hip dominant -- never do this the day before a quality run or long run. Place it on a rest or easy day with at least one day between it and your next hard effort
- Strength 2 is core and upper back dominant -- this one is more flexible. It can sit the day before a long run if needed, but avoid placing it before a quality speed or threshold session
- Mobility 1 always goes after your long run, before you sit down. The muscles are warm and this is when it does the most good
- Mobility 2 goes after any easy run during the week -- mid-week works best
- When in doubt, alternate through the week: Strength 1, Mobility 2, Strength 2, Mobility 1 -- never two of the same back to back

These are non-negotiable -- ten minutes of skipping them now can cost you weeks later. Trust the process.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Strength 1
upcoming.description:
Overview
Targets the muscles that keep your running form intact when you are tired -- glutes, hip abductors, and single-leg stability. Weakness here shows up as knee drift, hip drop, and lower back pain in the back half of races. Twenty minutes once a week will make a measurable difference over a full campaign.

When to do it
Do this on a rest or easy day, with at least one day between this session and your next quality run or long run. Never the day before a hard effort.

---

Glute Bridge -- 3x15
Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. Drive through your heels to lift your hips until your body forms a straight line from knees to shoulders. Squeeze your glutes at the top, hold for 1 second, then lower slowly.

Clamshell with Band -- 3x15 each side
Place the band just above your knees. Lie on your side with hips stacked, knees bent to 90 degrees. Keeping your feet together, rotate your top knee upward as far as you can without your hips rolling back. Lower with control. Keep your core braced throughout.

Lateral Band Walk -- 3x15 steps each direction
Place the band just above your ankles. Stand with feet hip-width apart and a slight bend in your knees. Step sideways, maintaining tension in the band at all times -- do not let your feet come together. Walk 15 steps one way, then return.

Bodyweight Squat -- 3x15
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes turned out slightly. Sit back and down as if lowering onto a chair, keeping your chest tall and knees tracking over your toes. Lower until thighs are parallel to the floor (or as low as comfortable), then drive back up through your heels.

Bird Dog -- 3x10 each side
Start on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips. Slowly extend your right arm forward and left leg back simultaneously, keeping your back flat and hips level. Hold for 2 seconds, return, then repeat on the other side. Move slowly -- control is everything here.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Mobility 2
upcoming.description:
Overview
Focuses on the calf-Achilles-foot complex and thoracic spine -- two areas runners consistently neglect until something goes wrong. Tight calves and stiff thoracic spine both reduce running economy and increase injury risk. The foot work with the spiky ball is particularly valuable if you carry any history of plantar fasciitis or Achilles tightness.

When to do it
Do this after an easy run during the week. Never skip this one if your calves or Achilles feel tight during a training block -- that tightness is the warning sign this session is designed to address.

---

Calf Roll -- 60s each leg  |  Foam roller
Sit on the floor with one calf resting on the roller. Lift yourself slightly on your hands so your weight presses into the roller. Roll slowly from ankle to just below the knee, rotating your leg slightly to cover the full calf.

Foot Roll -- 60s each foot  |  Spiky ball
Stand with the spiky ball under the arch of one foot. Apply as much bodyweight as comfortable and roll slowly from heel to ball of foot, pausing on tender spots. Hold a wall for balance if needed.

Thoracic Roll -- 60s  |  Foam roller
Sit with the roller across your mid-back just below your shoulder blades. Support your head with hands behind your neck. Let your upper back extend gently over the roller and move it up a few centimetres at a time. Mid and upper back only -- not the lower back.

Calf Stretch -- 2x45s each leg
Stand facing a wall with both hands on it. Step one foot back, keep that leg straight and press the heel firmly into the floor. Lean gently into the wall until you feel the stretch in the calf.

Soleus Stretch -- 2x45s each leg
Same position as the calf stretch but bend the back knee slightly while keeping the heel down. This targets the deeper calf muscle -- often tighter than the upper calf in runners and frequently missed.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: Easy Run — 40min
upcoming.description:
Phase: Post-Marathon Recovery — Week 2

- 40m Z1 HR

Stepping up 5 minutes from Monday. Heart rate first — slow down before you let it creep up. Coach Helmot.

upcoming.name: MSR Strength 2
upcoming.description:
Overview
Covers what Strength A does not -- your core, upper back, and calves. These are the muscles that hold your posture together in the final kilometres when everything wants to collapse forward. The upper back work is especially important: strong rhomboids and mid-traps keep your arm drive efficient and your chest open when you are fatigued.

When to do it
Do this on an easy day between your two hardest sessions of the week. It is core and upper body dominant so it will not affect your legs, making it the most flexible of the four sessions to place.

---

Single-Leg Calf Raise -- 3x15 each leg
Stand near a wall for balance. Lift one foot slightly off the ground and rise up slowly onto the toes of your standing foot. Lower with control -- do not drop. If this is easy, do it from the edge of a step for a greater range of motion.

Side Plank -- 3x30s each side
Lie on your side and prop yourself up on your forearm, elbow directly under your shoulder. Stack your feet or stagger them for balance. Lift your hips so your body forms a straight line from head to feet. Hold. If too hard, drop your bottom knee to the floor.

Band Pull-Apart -- 3x15
Hold the band at chest height with both hands, arms straight and shoulder-width apart. Pull the band apart by squeezing your shoulder blades together until your arms form a T shape. Hold for 1 second at the end, then return slowly. Keep your elbows straight throughout.

Banded Row -- 3x12 each side
Anchor the band around a door handle or post at waist height. Hold one end, step back until there is tension, and stand tall. Pull your elbow straight back, squeezing your shoulder blade at the end. Return slowly. Complete all reps on one side before switching.

Superman -- 3x10
Lie face down with arms extended overhead. Simultaneously lift your arms, chest, and legs off the floor, squeezing your glutes and lower back. Hold for 2 seconds at the top, then lower slowly. Keep the movement controlled -- this is not about height, it is about tension.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Mobility 1
upcoming.description:
Overview
Targets everything that tightens up on long run day -- quads, glutes, hip flexors, and hamstrings. Done consistently, it reduces the stiffness that builds across a training block and keeps your stride length and hip extension where they need to be.

When to do it
Do this immediately after your long run or race, before you sit down. The muscles are warm and more receptive to stretching. If that is not possible, do it within a few hours -- but always on the same day.

---

Quad Roll -- 60s each leg  |  Foam roller
Lie face down with the roller under one thigh just above the knee. Support yourself on your forearms and slowly roll up toward your hip, pausing on tight spots. Switch legs.

Glute Roll -- 60s each side  |  Foam roller
Sit on the roller under one glute. Cross that ankle over your opposite knee to open the hip. Lean slightly toward the side you are rolling and move slowly, pausing where it is tender.

Hip Flexor Lunge Stretch -- 2x45s each side
Kneel on one knee with the other foot forward at 90 degrees. Tuck your pelvis under slightly, then shift your weight forward until you feel a stretch in the front of your back hip. Keep your torso upright. Switch sides.

Pigeon Pose -- 2x60s each side
From kneeling, bring one knee forward and angle your shin across your body. Extend the back leg straight behind you. Lower your hips toward the floor and walk your hands forward if comfortable. You should feel this deep in the outer hip and glute.

Hamstring Stretch with Band -- 2x45s each leg
Lie on your back. Loop the band around the sole of one foot and gently pull your straight leg toward you until you feel a stretch in the back of the thigh. Keep the other leg flat on the floor. Do not force -- let the tension build gradually.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: Coach Note -- MSR Strength & Mobility
upcoming.description:
New This Week: MSR Strength & Mobility Sessions

You'll notice four new recurring sessions in your training plan -- two strength and two mobility. These are part of your marathon preparation and are designed to be done alongside your running, not instead of it.

WHY STRENGTH?
The two strength sessions (MSR Strength 1 and MSR Strength 2) target the muscles that keep your form intact when you're tired -- glutes, hips, core, and upper back. These are the things that break down in the back half of a race: knee drift, hip drop, collapsed posture, shortened stride. Twenty minutes of consistent work here will make a real difference come race day.

WHY MOBILITY?
The two mobility sessions (MSR Mobility 1 and MSR Mobility 2) focus on the areas runners chronically neglect -- hip flexors, glutes, calves, feet, and thoracic spine. Tightness in these areas reduces your running economy and increases injury risk over a long campaign. Done consistently, this work keeps you moving well and recovering faster between sessions.

TECHNIQUE MATTERS MORE THAN LOAD
These sessions are not about how heavy, how many, or how fast. They are about control. A half-hearted glute bridge does nothing -- a slow, deliberate one with a proper squeeze at the top is worth ten sloppy ones. Read the exercise descriptions carefully, move with intention, and if something doesn't feel right, slow down further. The benefits come from quality of movement, not quantity of effort.

WHAT YOU NEED
A resistance band, a foam roller, and a spiky ball. That's it.

WHEN TO DO THEM

- Strength 2 is core and upper back dominant -- this one is more flexible. It can sit the day before a long run if needed, but avoid placing it before a quality speed or threshold session
- Mobility 1 always goes after your long run, before you sit down. The muscles are warm and this is when it does the most good
- Mobility 2 goes after any easy run during the week -- mid-week works best
- When in doubt, alternate through the week: Strength 1, Mobility 2, Strength 2, Mobility 1 -- never two of the same back to back

These are non-negotiable -- ten minutes of skipping them now can cost you weeks later. Trust the process.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Strength 2
upcoming.description:
Overview
Covers what Strength A does not -- your core, upper back, and calves. These are the muscles that hold your posture together in the final kilometres when everything wants to collapse forward. The upper back work is especially important: strong rhomboids and mid-traps keep your arm drive efficient and your chest open when you are fatigued.

When to do it
Do this on an easy day between your two hardest sessions of the week. It is core and upper body dominant so it will not affect your legs, making it the most flexible of the four sessions to place.

---

Single-Leg Calf Raise -- 3x15 each leg
Stand near a wall for balance. Lift one foot slightly off the ground and rise up slowly onto the toes of your standing foot. Lower with control -- do not drop. If this is easy, do it from the edge of a step for a greater range of motion.

Side Plank -- 3x30s each side
Lie on your side and prop yourself up on your forearm, elbow directly under your shoulder. Stack your feet or stagger them for balance. Lift your hips so your body forms a straight line from head to feet. Hold. If too hard, drop your bottom knee to the floor.

Band Pull-Apart -- 3x15
Hold the band at chest height with both hands, arms straight and shoulder-width apart. Pull the band apart by squeezing your shoulder blades together until your arms form a T shape. Hold for 1 second at the end, then return slowly. Keep your elbows straight throughout.

Banded Row -- 3x12 each side
Anchor the band around a door handle or post at waist height. Hold one end, step back until there is tension, and stand tall. Pull your elbow straight back, squeezing your shoulder blade at the end. Return slowly. Complete all reps on one side before switching.

Superman -- 3x10
Lie face down with arms extended overhead. Simultaneously lift your arms, chest, and legs off the floor, squeezing your glutes and lower back. Hold for 2 seconds at the top, then lower slowly. Keep the movement controlled -- this is not about height, it is about tension.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Mobility 1
upcoming.description:
Overview
Targets everything that tightens up on long run day -- quads, glutes, hip flexors, and hamstrings. Done consistently, it reduces the stiffness that builds across a training block and keeps your stride length and hip extension where they need to be.

When to do it
Do this immediately after your long run or race, before you sit down. The muscles are warm and more receptive to stretching. If that is not possible, do it within a few hours -- but always on the same day.

---

Quad Roll -- 60s each leg  |  Foam roller
Lie face down with the roller under one thigh just above the knee. Support yourself on your forearms and slowly roll up toward your hip, pausing on tight spots. Switch legs.

Glute Roll -- 60s each side  |  Foam roller
Sit on the roller under one glute. Cross that ankle over your opposite knee to open the hip. Lean slightly toward the side you are rolling and move slowly, pausing where it is tender.

Hip Flexor Lunge Stretch -- 2x45s each side
Kneel on one knee with the other foot forward at 90 degrees. Tuck your pelvis under slightly, then shift your weight forward until you feel a stretch in the front of your back hip. Keep your torso upright. Switch sides.

Pigeon Pose -- 2x60s each side
From kneeling, bring one knee forward and angle your shin across your body. Extend the back leg straight behind you. Lower your hips toward the floor and walk your hands forward if comfortable. You should feel this deep in the outer hip and glute.

Hamstring Stretch with Band -- 2x45s each leg
Lie on your back. Loop the band around the sole of one foot and gently pull your straight leg toward you until you feel a stretch in the back of the thigh. Keep the other leg flat on the floor. Do not force -- let the tension build gradually.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: Coach Note -- MSR Strength & Mobility
upcoming.description:
New This Week: MSR Strength & Mobility Sessions

You'll notice four new recurring sessions in your training plan -- two strength and two mobility. These are part of your marathon preparation and are designed to be done alongside your running, not instead of it.

WHY STRENGTH?
The two strength sessions (MSR Strength 1 and MSR Strength 2) target the muscles that keep your form intact when you're tired -- glutes, hips, core, and upper back. These are the things that break down in the back half of a race: knee drift, hip drop, collapsed posture, shortened stride. Twenty minutes of consistent work here will make a real difference come race day.

WHY MOBILITY?
The two mobility sessions (MSR Mobility 1 and MSR Mobility 2) focus on the areas runners chronically neglect -- hip flexors, glutes, calves, feet, and thoracic spine. Tightness in these areas reduces your running economy and increases injury risk over a long campaign. Done consistently, this work keeps you moving well and recovering faster between sessions.

TECHNIQUE MATTERS MORE THAN LOAD
These sessions are not about how heavy, how many, or how fast. They are about control. A half-hearted glute bridge does nothing -- a slow, deliberate one with a proper squeeze at the top is worth ten sloppy ones. Read the exercise descriptions carefully, move with intention, and if something doesn't feel right, slow down further. The benefits come from quality of movement, not quantity of effort.

WHAT YOU NEED
A resistance band, a foam roller, and a spiky ball. That's it.

WHEN TO DO THEM
The sessions are placed in your calendar to fit around your running each week, but if your schedule changes keep these principles in mind:

- Strength 1 is glute and hip dominant -- never do this the day before a quality run or long run. Place it on a rest or easy day with at least one day between it and your next hard effort
- Strength 2 is core and upper back dominant -- this one is more flexible. It can sit the day before a long run if needed, but avoid placing it before a quality speed or threshold session
- Mobility 1 always goes after your long run, before you sit down. The muscles are warm and this is when it does the most good
- Mobility 2 goes after any easy run during the week -- mid-week works best
- When in doubt, alternate through the week: Strength 1, Mobility 2, Strength 2, Mobility 1 -- never two of the same back to back

These are non-negotiable -- ten minutes of skipping them now can cost you weeks later. Trust the process.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Strength 1
upcoming.description:
Overview
Targets the muscles that keep your running form intact when you are tired -- glutes, hip abductors, and single-leg stability. Weakness here shows up as knee drift, hip drop, and lower back pain in the back half of races. Twenty minutes once a week will make a measurable difference over a full campaign.

When to do it
Do this on a rest or easy day, with at least one day between this session and your next quality run or long run. Never the day before a hard effort.

---

Glute Bridge -- 3x15
Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. Drive through your heels to lift your hips until your body forms a straight line from knees to shoulders. Squeeze your glutes at the top, hold for 1 second, then lower slowly.

Clamshell with Band -- 3x15 each side
Place the band just above your knees. Lie on your side with hips stacked, knees bent to 90 degrees. Keeping your feet together, rotate your top knee upward as far as you can without your hips rolling back. Lower with control. Keep your core braced throughout.

Lateral Band Walk -- 3x15 steps each direction
Place the band just above your ankles. Stand with feet hip-width apart and a slight bend in your knees. Step sideways, maintaining tension in the band at all times -- do not let your feet come together. Walk 15 steps one way, then return.

Bodyweight Squat -- 3x15
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes turned out slightly. Sit back and down as if lowering onto a chair, keeping your chest tall and knees tracking over your toes. Lower until thighs are parallel to the floor (or as low as comfortable), then drive back up through your heels.

Bird Dog -- 3x10 each side
Start on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips. Slowly extend your right arm forward and left leg back simultaneously, keeping your back flat and hips level. Hold for 2 seconds, return, then repeat on the other side. Move slowly -- control is everything here.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Mobility 2
upcoming.description:
Overview
Focuses on the calf-Achilles-foot complex and thoracic spine -- two areas runners consistently neglect until something goes wrong. Tight calves and stiff thoracic spine both reduce running economy and increase injury risk. The foot work with the spiky ball is particularly valuable if you carry any history of plantar fasciitis or Achilles tightness.

When to do it
Do this after an easy run during the week. Never skip this one if your calves or Achilles feel tight during a training block -- that tightness is the warning sign this session is designed to address.

---

Calf Roll -- 60s each leg  |  Foam roller
Sit on the floor with one calf resting on the roller. Lift yourself slightly on your hands so your weight presses into the roller. Roll slowly from ankle to just below the knee, rotating your leg slightly to cover the full calf.

Foot Roll -- 60s each foot  |  Spiky ball
Stand with the spiky ball under the arch of one foot. Apply as much bodyweight as comfortable and roll slowly from heel to ball of foot, pausing on tender spots. Hold a wall for balance if needed.

Thoracic Roll -- 60s  |  Foam roller
Sit with the roller across your mid-back just below your shoulder blades. Support your head with hands behind your neck. Let your upper back extend gently over the roller and move it up a few centimetres at a time. Mid and upper back only -- not the lower back.

Calf Stretch -- 2x45s each leg
Stand facing a wall with both hands on it. Step one foot back, keep that leg straight and press the heel firmly into the floor. Lean gently into the wall until you feel the stretch in the calf.

Soleus Stretch -- 2x45s each leg
Same position as the calf stretch but bend the back knee slightly while keeping the heel down. This targets the deeper calf muscle -- often tighter than the upper calf in runners and frequently missed.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Strength 1
upcoming.description:
Overview
Targets the muscles that keep your running form intact when you are tired -- glutes, hip abductors, and single-leg stability. Weakness here shows up as knee drift, hip drop, and lower back pain in the back half of races. Twenty minutes once a week will make a measurable difference over a full campaign.

When to do it
Do this on a rest or easy day, with at least one day between this session and your next quality run or long run. Never the day before a hard effort.

---

Glute Bridge -- 3x15
Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. Drive through your heels to lift your hips until your body forms a straight line from knees to shoulders. Squeeze your glutes at the top, hold for 1 second, then lower slowly.

Clamshell with Band -- 3x15 each side
Place the band just above your knees. Lie on your side with hips stacked, knees bent to 90 degrees. Keeping your feet together, rotate your top knee upward as far as you can without your hips rolling back. Lower with control. Keep your core braced throughout.

Lateral Band Walk -- 3x15 steps each direction
Place the band just above your ankles. Stand with feet hip-width apart and a slight bend in your knees. Step sideways, maintaining tension in the band at all times -- do not let your feet come together. Walk 15 steps one way, then return.

Bodyweight Squat -- 3x15
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes turned out slightly. Sit back and down as if lowering onto a chair, keeping your chest tall and knees tracking over your toes. Lower until thighs are parallel to the floor (or as low as comfortable), then drive back up through your heels.

Bird Dog -- 3x10 each side
Start on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips. Slowly extend your right arm forward and left leg back simultaneously, keeping your back flat and hips level. Hold for 2 seconds, return, then repeat on the other side. Move slowly -- control is everything here.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Mobility 2
upcoming.description:
Overview
Focuses on the calf-Achilles-foot complex and thoracic spine -- two areas runners consistently neglect until something goes wrong. Tight calves and stiff thoracic spine both reduce running economy and increase injury risk. The foot work with the spiky ball is particularly valuable if you carry any history of plantar fasciitis or Achilles tightness.

When to do it
Do this after an easy run during the week. Never skip this one if your calves or Achilles feel tight during a training block -- that tightness is the warning sign this session is designed to address.

---

Calf Roll -- 60s each leg  |  Foam roller
Sit on the floor with one calf resting on the roller. Lift yourself slightly on your hands so your weight presses into the roller. Roll slowly from ankle to just below the knee, rotating your leg slightly to cover the full calf.

Foot Roll -- 60s each foot  |  Spiky ball
Stand with the spiky ball under the arch of one foot. Apply as much bodyweight as comfortable and roll slowly from heel to ball of foot, pausing on tender spots. Hold a wall for balance if needed.

upcoming.name: MSR Strength 2

Overview
Covers what Strength A does not -- your core, upper back, and calves. These are the muscles that hold your posture together in the final kilometres when everything wants to collapse forward. The upper back work is especially important: strong rhomboids and mid-traps keep your arm drive efficient and your chest open when you are fatigued.

When to do it
Do this on an easy day between your two hardest sessions of the week. It is core and upper body dominant so it will not affect your legs, making it the most flexible of the four sessions to place.

---

Single-Leg Calf Raise -- 3x15 each leg
Stand near a wall for balance. Lift one foot slightly off the ground and rise up slowly onto the toes of your standing foot. Lower with control -- do not drop. If this is easy, do it from the edge of a step for a greater range of motion.

Side Plank -- 3x30s each side
Lie on your side and prop yourself up on your forearm, elbow directly under your shoulder. Stack your feet or stagger them for balance. Lift your hips so your body forms a straight line from head to feet. Hold. If too hard, drop your bottom knee to the floor.

Band Pull-Apart -- 3x15

Banded Row -- 3x12 each side
Anchor the band around a door handle or post at waist height. Hold one end, step back until there is tension, and stand tall. Pull your elbow straight back, squeezing your shoulder blade at the end. Return slowly. Complete all reps on one side before switching.

Superman -- 3x10
Lie face down with arms extended overhead. Simultaneously lift your arms, chest, and legs off the floor, squeezing your glutes and lower back. Hold for 2 seconds at the top, then lower slowly. Keep the movement controlled -- this is not about height, it is about tension.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Mobility 1
upcoming.description:
Overview
Targets everything that tightens up on long run day -- quads, glutes, hip flexors, and hamstrings. Done consistently, it reduces the stiffness that builds across a training block and keeps your stride length and hip extension where they need to be.

When to do it
Do this immediately after your long run or race, before you sit down. The muscles are warm and more receptive to stretching. If that is not possible, do it within a few hours -- but always on the same day.

---

Quad Roll -- 60s each leg  |  Foam roller
Lie face down with the roller under one thigh just above the knee. Support yourself on your forearms and slowly roll up toward your hip, pausing on tight spots. Switch legs.

Glute Roll -- 60s each side  |  Foam roller
Sit on the roller under one glute. Cross that ankle over your opposite knee to open the hip. Lean slightly toward the side you are rolling and move slowly, pausing where it is tender.

Hip Flexor Lunge Stretch -- 2x45s each side
Kneel on one knee with the other foot forward at 90 degrees. Tuck your pelvis under slightly, then shift your weight forward until you feel a stretch in the front of your back hip. Keep your torso upright. Switch sides.

Pigeon Pose -- 2x60s each side
From kneeling, bring one knee forward and angle your shin across your body. Extend the back leg straight behind you. Lower your hips toward the floor and walk your hands forward if comfortable. You should feel this deep in the outer hip and glute.

Hamstring Stretch with Band -- 2x45s each leg
Lie on your back. Loop the band around the sole of one foot and gently pull your straight leg toward you until you feel a stretch in the back of the thigh. Keep the other leg flat on the floor. Do not force -- let the tension build gradually.

Coach Helmot

Do this immediately after your long run or race, before you sit down. The muscles are warm and more receptive to stretching. If that is not possible, do it within a few hours -- but always on the same day.

---

Quad Roll -- 60s each leg  |  Foam roller
Lie face down with the roller under one thigh just above the knee. Support yourself on your forearms and slowly roll up toward your hip, pausing on tight spots. Switch legs.

Glute Roll -- 60s each side  |  Foam roller
Sit on the roller under one glute. Cross that ankle over your opposite knee to open the hip. Lean slightly toward the side you are rolling and move slowly, pausing where it is tender.

Hip Flexor Lunge Stretch -- 2x45s each side
Kneel on one knee with the other foot forward at 90 degrees. Tuck your pelvis under slightly, then shift your weight forward until you feel a stretch in the front of your back hip. Keep your torso upright. Switch sides.

Pigeon Pose -- 2x60s each side
From kneeling, bring one knee forward and angle your shin across your body. Extend the back leg straight behind you. Lower your hips toward the floor and walk your hands forward if comfortable. You should feel this deep in the outer hip and glute.

Hamstring Stretch with Band -- 2x45s each leg
Lie on your back. Loop the band around the sole of one foot and gently pull your straight leg toward you until you feel a stretch in the back of the thigh. Keep the other leg flat on the floor. Do not force -- let the tension build gradually.

Coach Helmot

Place the band just above your ankles. Stand with feet hip-width apart and a slight bend in your knees. Step sideways, maintaining tension in the band at all times -- do not let your feet come together. Walk 15 steps one way, then return.

upcoming.name: Coach Note -- MSR Strength & Mobility
upcoming.description:
New This Week: MSR Strength & Mobility Sessions

You'll notice four new recurring sessions in your training plan -- two strength and two mobility. These are part of your marathon preparation and are designed to be done alongside your running, not instead of it.

WHY STRENGTH?
The two strength sessions (MSR Strength 1 and MSR Strength 2) target the muscles that keep your form intact when you're tired -- glutes, hips, core, and upper back. These are the things that break down in the back half of a race: knee drift, hip drop, collapsed posture, shortened stride. Twenty minutes of consistent work here will make a real difference come race day.

WHY MOBILITY?
The two mobility sessions (MSR Mobility 1 and MSR Mobility 2) focus on the areas runners chronically neglect -- hip flexors, glutes, calves, feet, and thoracic spine. Tightness in these areas reduces your running economy and increases injury risk over a long campaign. Done consistently, this work keeps you moving well and recovering faster between sessions.

TECHNIQUE MATTERS MORE THAN LOAD
These sessions are not about how heavy, how many, or how fast. They are about control. A half-hearted glute bridge does nothing -- a slow, deliberate one with a proper squeeze at the top is worth ten sloppy ones. Read the exercise descriptions carefully, move with intention, and if something doesn't feel right, slow down further. The benefits come from quality of movement, not quantity of effort.

WHAT YOU NEED
A resistance band, a foam roller, and a spiky ball. That's it.

WHEN TO DO THEM
The sessions are placed in your calendar to fit around your running each week, but if your schedule changes keep these principles in mind:

- Strength 1 is glute and hip dominant -- never do this the day before a quality run or long run. Place it on a rest or easy day with at least one day between it and your next hard effort
- Strength 2 is core and upper back dominant -- this one is more flexible. It can sit the day before a long run if needed, but avoid placing it before a quality speed or threshold session
- Mobility 1 always goes after your long run, before you sit down. The muscles are warm and this is when it does the most good
- Mobility 2 goes after any easy run during the week -- mid-week works best
- When in doubt, alternate through the week: Strength 1, Mobility 2, Strength 2, Mobility 1 -- never two of the same back to back

These are non-negotiable -- ten minutes of skipping them now can cost you weeks later. Trust the process.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Mobility 2
upcoming.description:
Overview
Focuses on the calf-Achilles-foot complex and thoracic spine -- two areas runners consistently neglect until something goes wrong. Tight calves and stiff thoracic spine both reduce running economy and increase injury risk. The foot work with the spiky ball is particularly valuable if you carry any history of plantar fasciitis or Achilles tightness.

When to do it
Do this after an easy run during the week. Never skip this one if your calves or Achilles feel tight during a training block -- that tightness is the warning sign this session is designed to address.

---

Calf Roll -- 60s each leg  |  Foam roller
Sit on the floor with one calf resting on the roller. Lift yourself slightly on your hands so your weight presses into the roller. Roll slowly from ankle to just below the knee, rotating your leg slightly to cover the full calf.

Foot Roll -- 60s each foot  |  Spiky ball
Stand with the spiky ball under the arch of one foot. Apply as much bodyweight as comfortable and roll slowly from heel to ball of foot, pausing on tender spots. Hold a wall for balance if needed.

Thoracic Roll -- 60s  |  Foam roller
Sit with the roller across your mid-back just below your shoulder blades. Support your head with hands behind your neck. Let your upper back extend gently over the roller and move it up a few centimetres at a time. Mid and upper back only -- not the lower back.

Calf Stretch -- 2x45s each leg
Stand facing a wall with both hands on it. Step one foot back, keep that leg straight and press the heel firmly into the floor. Lean gently into the wall until you feel the stretch in the calf.

Soleus Stretch -- 2x45s each leg
Same position as the calf stretch but bend the back knee slightly while keeping the heel down. This targets the deeper calf muscle -- often tighter than the upper calf in runners and frequently missed.

Coach Helmot

upcoming.name: MSR Strength 2
upcoming.description:
Overview
Covers what Strength A does not -- your core, upper back, and calves. These are the muscles that hold your posture together in the final kilometres when everything wants to collapse forward. The upper back work is especially important: strong rhomboids and mid-traps keep your arm drive efficient and your chest open when you are fatigued.

When to do it
Do this on an easy day between your two hardest sessions of the week. It is core and upper body dominant so it will not affect your legs, making it the most flexible of the four sessions to place.

---

Single-Leg Calf Raise -- 3x15 each leg
Stand near a wall for balance. Lift one foot slightly off the ground and rise up slowly onto the toes of your standing foot. Lower with control -- do not drop. If this is easy, do it from the edge of a step for a greater range of motion.

Side Plank -- 3x30s each side
Lie on your side and prop yourself up on your forearm, elbow directly under your shoulder. Stack your feet or stagger them for balance. Lift your hips so your body forms a straight line from head to feet. Hold. If too hard, drop your bottom knee to the floor.

Band Pull-Apart -- 3x15
Hold the band at chest height with both hands, arms straight and shoulder-width apart. Pull the band apart by squeezing your shoulder blades together until your arms form a T shape. Hold for 1 second at the end, then return slowly. Keep your elbows straight throughout.

Banded Row -- 3x12 each side
Anchor the band around a door handle or post at waist height. Hold one end, step back until there is tension, and stand tall. Pull your elbow straight back, squeezing your shoulder blade at the end. Return slowly. Complete all reps on one side before switching.

Superman -- 3x10
Lie face down with arms extended overhead. Simultaneously lift your arms, chest, and legs off the floor, squeezing your glutes and lower back. Hold for 2 seconds at the top, then lower slowly. Keep the movement controlled -- this is not about height, it is about tension.

Coach Helmot
